text: Hello, my name is Ara Kashishian and I would like to review a robotic Lysol adhesion for a patient who presented to our office with a long standing abdominal pain. This had been worked up with CT scans which on one or two occasions had shown some dilated loops of bowel.
The patient has several small incisions on the abdomen where trocars are inserted, allowing robotic instruments to be placed into the abdominal cavity once the robot is connected.

to the patient the benefit and this the difference between robot and the laparoscopic approach is that though in this case as you can appreciate the robot allows articulation of the tip of the instrument essentially at a three hundred sixty degree angle as you can see the scissors on the top of the image it's essentially bent and allows me to cut at different angle without really mobilizing the instrumentation in and out of the patient. As a surgeon I also have full control of the camera where I can toggle the control of the one of the four arms that has a camera to allow me to get closer and or further away and change the angle.

The surgeon uses articulated scissors to dissect and cauterize scar tissue, separating loops of the small bowel and surrounding fatty tissue from the anterior abdominal wall. The robotic instruments allow precise manipulation without moving in and out of the patient, effectively relieving the partial bowel obstruction.

With laparoscopic approach, this robotic approach also allows us to do this with three or four different small incisions and they all heal relatively quickly. And I think this is coming to the end and that little bleeding is cauterized and the case is over here shortly. Thank you, have a good day.
